questions.questionText: Spot the part of the sentence that contains a grammatical error: "One of the students (A) / who was selected for the debate (B) / have not submitted (C) / the consent form yet. (D)"
questions.explanation: The subject of the main clause is 'One', which is singular, so the verb must be 'has not submitted', not 'have not submitted'. The error therefore lies in Part C.

questions.questionText: Identify the segment of the sentence that has an error: "The committee (A) / discussed about the proposal (B) / for more than two hours (C) / before reaching a decision. (D)"
questions.explanation: The verb 'discuss' is transitive and takes a direct object without a preposition; 'discussed about the proposal' should simply be 'discussed the proposal'. Hence the error is in Part B.

questions.questionText: Find the part of the sentence containing an error: "Neither the captain (A) / nor the players (B) / was aware of (C) / the sudden change in the schedule. (D)"
questions.explanation: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject nearer to it. Since 'the players' is plural, the verb should be 'were aware of', making Part C incorrect.

questions.questionText: Choose the correct option to fill in the blank: "If she _____ harder last year, she would have cleared the examination."
questions.explanation: This is a third conditional sentence describing an unreal past situation. The if-clause requires the past perfect 'had worked' to pair with 'would have cleared' in the main clause.

questions.questionText: Select the most appropriate word to complete the sentence: "The new policy will come _____ effect from the first of next month."
questions.explanation: The fixed expression is 'come into effect', meaning to become operational or valid. 'Into' indicates the transition from one state to another, which suits this idiom.

questions.questionText: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: "Hardly had the train left the station _____ it started raining heavily."
questions.explanation: The correct correlative structure is 'Hardly had...when', not 'than'. 'No sooner' pairs with 'than', whereas 'hardly' and 'scarcely' pair with 'when'.

questions.questionText: Choose the option that improves the underlined part of the sentence. If no improvement is needed, select 'No improvement': "She is senior than me by three years in this organisation."
questions.explanation: Latin-origin comparatives such as 'senior', 'junior', 'superior', and 'inferior' take the preposition 'to' instead of 'than'. The correct form is 'senior to me'.

questions.questionText: Select the best improvement for the underlined part of the sentence. If the sentence is correct as it is, choose 'No improvement': "The manager insisted that the report is submitted before the meeting began."
questions.explanation: Verbs of demand, suggestion, or insistence such as 'insist' are followed by the subjunctive mood, which uses the base form of the verb. Hence 'insisted that the report be submitted' is correct.

questions.questionText: Choose the option that best replaces the underlined part of the sentence. If no change is required, select 'No improvement': "Despite of repeated warnings, the workers continued to ignore the safety guidelines."
questions.explanation: 'Despite' is never followed by 'of'; the correct alternatives are 'despite repeated warnings' or 'in spite of repeated warnings'. The first option fixes the error correctly.
